interactions.summary: The prior student exchange trails off; Jiang asks for an example, thanks the student, and opens the room for more questions.
A student asks whether it matters who first has the vision to build something and whether a second person must have the same vision, or whether the shared aim of bringing heaven to earth is enough.

Jiang answers that ancient monumental work did not depend on individual ownership, patents, credit, or private property; what mattered was expressing a shared vision together, and that common-sacrifice mentality makes tremendous collective work possible.

speaker_notes.note: Segment 0067 is a clear student question about shared vision and individual originators.

claims.claim: Jiang says ancient monumental work was not organized around individual proprietary credit; it was organized around whether the shared vision was built.
claims.temporal_scope: answer to student question in this lecture

claims.claim: For Jiang, the desire to patent, get credit, and monetize an idea is part of why modern ideas become weak.
claims.temporal_scope: modern creativity critique in this lecture

claims.claim: He describes inspiration as arriving when people share who they are; if the motive is only money, God leaves and the ideas stop coming.
Common sacrifice and lack of individualism, capitalism, and private-property orientation are presented as the mentality that makes tremendous collective projects possible.

signature_moments.moment: The pyramid has no author because authorship is beside the point: all that matters is that it was built.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Why? Because it doesn't matter who built the pyramids. All that matters is it was built.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This answers the student's question by dissolving modern authorship into shared sacred accomplishment.

signature_moments.moment: God gives ideas to the person who shares who they are; money-mindedness makes God leave.
signature_moments.source_phrase: God comes into you and gives you ideas
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes Jiang's creativity model explicitly theological and anti-proprietary.

signature_moments.moment: Common sacrifice is the lost mentality that makes tremendous things possible together.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the closing public thesis of the episode: heaven on earth is a collective discipline, not a technology stack.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The shared mentality Jiang says allows people to achieve tremendous things together.
A collective relation to ideas where credit, patents, money, and private ownership do not matter as much as manifesting the shared vision.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture closes by contrasting ancient common sacrifice with contemporary examples such as ChatGPT, which Jiang dismisses as not a comparable wonder.

uncertainty_notes.note: The short hesitant exchange before the final student question is not substantive enough to treat as a public question.
